threats.title: Genesis Depositors Can Steal Historical Gauge Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_depositsForGenesis` function updates global reward state but fails to update the depositor's personal reward checkpoint, allowing them to claim rewards retroactively from the beginning of the gauge's lifetime.
threats.investigation_rationale: When a user deposits into a gauge, their `userRewardPerTokenPaid` must be set to the current `rewardPerTokenStored` so they only earn rewards from that point forward. `_depositsForGenesis` calls `updateReward(address(0))` instead of `updateReward(_tokenOwner)`, leaving the depositor's checkpoint at 0. This allows them to steal rewards meant for earlier stakers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateReward(address(0)) modifier used instead of updateReward(_tokenOwner)

preconditions: Gauge has accumulated rewards prior to the genesis deposit
Genesis pool calls depositsForGenesis for a user

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing user state update during deposit

analysis_questions: Can a user trigger a genesis deposit after rewards have started accumulating?
Does the genesis pool allow arbitrary users to deposit at any time?
validation_requirements: Simulate a gauge with existing rewards and trigger a genesis deposit
Verify if the new depositor can immediately claim historical rewards

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A gauge is active and distributing rewards. A user deposits via the genesis pool. The gauge calls `depositsForGenesis`. The user immediately calls `getReward()` and receives rewards calculated from `rewardPerTokenStored - 0`, stealing funds from the gauge.
prerequisites: Ability to deposit via genesis pool

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of accumulated gauge rewards by late depositors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Legitimate gauge stakers who lose their yield

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for genesis deposits occurring after reward distribution has started

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Requires capital to deposit into genesis pool
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via disproportionate reward claims

threats.title: Arbitrary Pair Transfer in Router Allows Draining Excess Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: The `swapExactTokensForTokens` function transfers user input tokens to a user-controlled address (`routes[0].pair`) but executes the swap on the legitimate pair address, allowing attackers to steal excess pool balances for free.
threats.investigation_rationale: The router trusts the user-provided `routes[0].pair` for the initial `_safeTransferFrom`, but `_swap` calculates the actual pair address using `pairFor`. An attacker can set `routes[0].pair` to their own address, keeping their input tokens, while the legitimate pair executes the swap using its excess balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeTransferFrom uses user-controlled routes[0].pair instead of computed pair address

preconditions: A legitimate pair has excess balance (e.g., from direct transfers or accumulated fees not yet synced)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Trusting user input for token destination
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Input tokens are sent to routes[0].pair, but swap is executed on pairFor(...)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Initial transfer should go to pairFor(routes[0].from, routes[0].to, routes[0].stable)

analysis_questions: How often do pairs accumulate excess balances?
Can an attacker artificially create excess balance to exploit this?
validation_requirements: Deploy a pair and send tokens directly to it
Verify attacker receives output tokens without losing input tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies a pair with excess balance. They call `swapExactTokensForTokens` with `routes[0].pair` set to their own wallet. The router transfers their input tokens back to them. `_swap` then calls the real pair, which uses its excess balance to fulfill the swap, sending output tokens to the attacker.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all excess balances across all pairs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol and LPs (if excess balances were meant to be synced)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for malicious route structs with mismatched pair addresses

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal, just enough to trigger the swap
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via token transfers to non-pair addresses during swaps

threats.title: Uninitialized Route Structs in swapExactTokensForTokensSimple Causes Fund Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: The `swapExactTokensForTokensSimple` function instantiates a `route` struct in memory but fails to initialize the `pair` and `receiver` fields, causing them to default to `address(0)` and leading to lost funds or reverts.
threats.investigation_rationale: When `new route[](1)` is called, memory is allocated but `routes[0].pair` and `routes[0].receiver` are never assigned. `_swap` uses `routes[i].receiver` as the destination for tokens. Consequently, the output tokens are sent to `address(0)`, permanently losing the user's funds.
preconditions: User calls swapExactTokensForTokensSimple
amountOutMin is set to 0 (otherwise getAmountsOut reverts)

analysis_questions: Does getAmountsOut revert when routes[0].pair is address(0)?
Can concentrated liquidity swaps bypass the revert and lose funds?
validation_requirements: Verify if tokens are sent to address(0)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user or frontend uses `swapExactTokensForTokensSimple` for a concentrated liquidity swap. The function executes, but because `routes[0].receiver` is `address(0)`, the output tokens are burned.

threats.title: Router _swap Function Ignores Recipient Parameter Leading to Misrouted Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: The internal `_swap` function accepts a `_to` parameter but never uses it, instead relying entirely on `routes[i].receiver`. This breaks the expected API contract and can lead to funds being sent to `address(0)`.
threats.investigation_rationale: In standard Solidly forks, `_swap` uses `_to` for the final hop. Here, `_to` is ignored, and `routes[i].receiver` is passed directly to the pair's `swap` function. If a caller provides a valid `to` address but leaves `routes[i].receiver` uninitialized in calldata, their funds are sent to the zero address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Parameter _to is declared but never read in the function body

preconditions: User calls a swap function and relies on the `to` parameter
User does not explicitly set `routes[last].receiver`

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Use _to for the final hop if receiver is not set

analysis_questions: Do frontends correctly populate routes[i].receiver?
What happens in swapExactTokensForETH if receiver is not address(this)?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user calls `swapExactTokensForETH` with `to` as their wallet, but the frontend leaves `routes[last].receiver` as `address(0)`. The WETH is sent to `address(0)` instead of the router, causing the subsequent `wETH.withdraw` to fail or withdraw 0, losing the user's funds.
prerequisites: Frontend integration flaw or user mistake

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of user funds or stuck transactions

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying the `to` parameter is respected

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via failed ETH withdrawals or transfers to address(0)

threats.title: Incorrect CREATE2 Address Computation in TradeHelper Breaks Routing
threats.hypothesis_description: `TradeHelper._pairFor` computes pair addresses using the `PairFactory` address and an incorrect init code hash, whereas pairs are actually deployed by `PairGenerator` with constructor arguments included in the init code.
threats.investigation_rationale: CREATE2 address computation requires the exact deployer address and the keccak256 hash of the full initialization code (including ABI-encoded constructor arguments). `TradeHelper` uses `factory` instead of `pairGenerator` and uses `type(Pair).creationCode` without arguments. This guarantees that all computed addresses will be wrong.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect CREATE2 parameters (wrong deployer, missing constructor args in hash)

preconditions: Integrator uses TradeHelper or GlobalRouter for routing

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Computed address will never match actual deployed pair
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should query PairFactory.getPair instead of computing manually

analysis_questions: Are there any contracts actively relying on GlobalRouter or TradeHelper?
Can an attacker deploy a malicious contract at the incorrectly predicted address?
validation_requirements: Compare TradeHelper.pairFor output with PairFactory.getPair output

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user attempts to swap via `GlobalRouter`. The router uses `TradeHelper.pairFor` to find the pair address. It gets an empty address. The swap attempts to call `swap()` on an empty address, which succeeds silently (if no return data check) or reverts, causing total failure of the routing infrastructure.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service for routing, potential loss if silent calls succeed

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests comparing computed addresses with actual deployments

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via failed transactions in GlobalRouter

threats.title: Infinite Loop in TradeHelper getAmountsIn Due to Integer Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: The `getAmountsIn` function in `TradeHelper` uses an unsigned integer `i` in a decrementing loop with the condition `i >= 0`. When `i` reaches 0, `i--` underflows to `type(uint256).max`, causing an infinite loop.
threats.investigation_rationale: In Solidity >=0.8.0, arithmetic underflow reverts by default. However, in a `for` loop, if `i` is `uint`, `i >= 0` is always true. When `i` is 0, the loop body executes, then `i--` is evaluated. This will trigger a panic (0x11) due to underflow, immediately reverting the transaction. The function is completely broken.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--) with unsigned integer

preconditions: Caller invokes TradeHelper.getAmountsIn

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsigned integer underflow in loop condition
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Use signed integer or change loop condition

analysis_questions: Is getAmountsIn used by any critical on-chain components?
validation_requirements: Call getAmountsIn with a valid route
Verify it reverts with underflow panic

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any frontend or contract attempting to calculate required input amounts for an exact output swap will call `getAmountsIn`. The call will always revert, breaking the exact-output swap functionality for the entire protocol.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service for exact-output routing

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing basic unit tests for getAmountsIn

threats.title: Inconsistent Pool Selection in Fee-on-Transfer Swaps Causes K-Invariant Reverts
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens` function calculates expected output using `getAmountOut`, which optimizes across both stable and volatile pools. However, it executes the swap on the pool specified by `routes[i].stable`, leading to K-invariant failures if the optimal pool differs from the specified one.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a user specifies a stable route, but the volatile pool offers a better rate, `getAmountOut` returns the volatile pool's output amount. The router then calls `swap` on the stable pool, demanding the higher volatile output amount. The stable pool cannot provide this without violating its K-invariant, causing the transaction to revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Mismatch between pool used for output calculation and pool used for execution

preconditions: User swaps a fee-on-transfer token
Both stable and volatile pools exist for the pair
The pool type specified in the route is not the one offering the best rate

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Ignoring returned pool type from oracle function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Demanding too much output from the wrong pool
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use the specific pool's getAmountOut, not the global optimized one

analysis_questions: How often do FOT tokens have both stable and volatile pools?
Does this completely break FOT swaps for these pairs?
validation_requirements: Create stable and volatile pools for a FOT token with different prices
Execute a FOT swap routed through the worse-priced pool

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user attempts to sell a FOT token via the router. The transaction unexpectedly reverts with a 'K' error because the router calculated the output using the volatile pool but executed the swap on the stable pool.
prerequisites: Specific market conditions (price discrepancy between pool types)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service for FOT token swaps
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users must manually interact with the pair contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for FOT swaps when multiple pool types exist

threats.title: Incorrect Math in TradeHelper getAmountInStable Returns Wrong Input Amounts
threats.hypothesis_description: The `getAmountInStable` function calculates the required input amount by incorrectly adding the desired output to the reserve (`reserveOut + output`) instead of subtracting it, and uses the wrong decimals for conversion.
threats.investigation_rationale: To find the input required for a specific output, the new output reserve must be `reserveOut - output`. The code does `uint y_1 = reserveOut + output`. Furthermore, it converts the final amount using `decimalsOut` instead of `decimalsIn`. This guarantees the function returns completely incorrect values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: y_1 = reserveOut + output instead of reserveOut - output

preconditions: Integrator relies on TradeHelper for pricing or routing

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Calculates input to add tokens instead of remove them

analysis_questions: Is this function used by any on-chain logic that could lead to fund loss?
Does this only affect off-chain UI quoting?
validation_requirements: Verify the returned amount is mathematically incorrect

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A frontend uses `TradeHelper` to quote the required input for a stable swap. It displays a wildly incorrect value to the user. If an on-chain contract uses this for slippage calculation, it will set incorrect limits, leading to failed swaps or MEV exposure (though MEV is out of scope here, failed swaps remain a DoS).

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users relying on the quoting infrastructure

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing mathematical validation tests for stable pool quoting

threats.title: Precision Loss in Fee Accumulator Causes Dust Fee Loss for LPs
threats.hypothesis_description: In `Pair._update0` and `_update1`, the fee ratio is calculated as `amount * 1e18 / totalSupply`. For pools with large total supplies or small swap amounts, this ratio rounds down to 0, permanently losing the fees for LPs.
threats.investigation_rationale: If `amount * 1e18 < totalSupply`, integer division results in 0. The fee tokens are transferred to the `PairFees` contract, but `index0`/`index1` are not incremented. LPs can never claim these tokens, and they remain permanently stuck in the `PairFees` contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 _ratio = amount * 1e18 / totalSupply without remainder tracking

preconditions: Pool has a very large totalSupply (e.g., meme tokens)
Users make small swaps where fee * 1e18 < totalSupply

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Fees are transferred but not accounted for
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Accumulate remainder or use higher precision multiplier

analysis_questions: How much value is typically lost to dust in high-supply pools?
Can an attacker intentionally trigger this to trap fees?
validation_requirements: Execute a swap generating a fee of 1e5
Verify fee is transferred to PairFees but index does not increase

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: In a pool with a massive total supply, normal trading activity generates fees. Due to the small size of individual swap fees relative to the supply, the ratio calculates to 0. The fees accumulate in the `PairFees` contract but are mathematically inaccessible to the LPs.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gradual loss of yield for LPs

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing precision loss tests for fee accumulation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via growing unaccounted balances in PairFees contracts

threats.title: Asymmetric Pause Mechanism Allows Liquidity Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `Pair.swap` function checks the factory's `isPaused` flag, but `mint` and `burn` do not. This allows users to add or remove liquidity during an emergency pause, potentially manipulating the pool ratio.
threats.investigation_rationale: When a protocol is paused (usually due to an exploit or oracle failure), all state-changing operations should ideally be halted. Because `mint` and `burn` remain active, an attacker can add heavily one-sided liquidity at stale prices during the pause, positioning themselves to extract value the moment the protocol is unpaused.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Inconsistent application of pause modifier across state-changing functions

preconditions: Protocol is paused by the owner
External market prices have moved significantly during the pause

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Liquidity can be manipulated while trading is frozen
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Add pause check to mint and burn

analysis_questions: Can one-sided liquidity addition significantly alter the pool's internal price?
Is it profitable to manipulate the pool during a pause?
validation_requirements: Verify they succeed while swap fails

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The protocol pauses due to extreme market volatility. During the pause, an attacker adds liquidity at the stale on-chain price. When the protocol unpauses, the attacker immediately swaps against their own liquidity or removes it, extracting arbitrage value from the delayed price update.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Value extraction from existing LPs upon unpausing

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying pause behavior across all pair functions

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: High capital to move pool ratios
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via large mint/burn events while swaps are paused

threats.title: Unbounded Referral Fee Can DoS All Swaps
threats.hypothesis_description: The `PairFactory.setReferralFee` function lacks an upper bound check. If a compromised or malicious admin sets the fee > 10000, the fee calculation in `Pair` will underflow, permanently reverting all swaps.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `Pair._update0`, `_referralFee = amount * _maxRef / 10000`. If `_maxRef > 10000`, `_referralFee` exceeds `amount`. The subsequent `amount -= _referralFee` will trigger an arithmetic underflow, reverting the transaction. This allows a single admin action to completely halt trading across the entire protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing upper bound validation on fee setter

preconditions: FeeManager role is compromised or acts maliciously
MAX_REFERRAL_FEE is set > 10000

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Causes underflow in core trading logic

analysis_questions: Is the FeeManager role secured by a timelock or multisig?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker compromises the FeeManager key and calls `setReferralFee(10001)`. All subsequent swaps on all pairs instantly revert due to underflow, causing a complete protocol denial of service.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete halt of protocol trading revenue
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin to reset the fee to a valid value

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary tests for fee setters

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via admin transaction setting invalid fee

threats.title: Flawed Zero Address Check in setInternalBribe Can Break Fee Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setInternalBribe` function uses a tautological check `require(_int >= address(0))` which always passes. This allows the admin to accidentally set the internal bribe to the zero address, breaking LP fee distribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: In Solidity, all addresses are represented as `uint160`, so any address is `>= address(0)`. If the owner sets `internal_bribe` to `address(0)`, the `claimFees` function will attempt to call `notifyRewardAmount` on the zero address, which will revert, preventing anyone from claiming fees.
preconditions: Owner accidentally or maliciously sets internal_bribe to address(0)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Allows setting critical contract dependency to zero address

analysis_questions: Does the factory ever deploy gauges with address(0) as the internal bribe?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The owner makes a configuration mistake and sets the internal bribe to `address(0)`. When the gauge attempts to distribute accumulated fees via `claimFees`, the transaction reverts, locking the fees until the owner fixes the configuration.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can fix by setting a valid address

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing negative tests for zero address validation
